pi_items.verified_value: Valid Windows path for STL export output
pi_items.source: Task specification analysis; path format matches Blue Sky Plan conventions

pi_items.verified_value: 30.0 mm is lower than typical mandible ramus height (40-71 mm range from literature) but could accommodate variation
pi_items.source: PubMed study on mandibular morphometry; literature shows ramus height typically 40-71mm, but minimum 30mm provides safety margin

pi_items.verified_value: 90.0 mm exceeds typical single mandible measurements (40-71 mm) and exceeds available DICOM scan volume z-depth (40 mm)
pi_items.source: DICOM analysis: scan volume is 102.4 x 102.4 x 40.0 mm; Literature: mandibular ramus height typically 40-71 mm; verifier comments indicate 40-80mm for mandible only

pi_items.verified_value: 500 KB minimum corresponds to approximately 10,000 triangles in binary STL format
pi_items.source: STL binary format analysis: 50 bytes per triangle means 500KB = ~10,000 triangles; literature indicates high-resolution mandible meshes contain 200,000+ triangles

pi_items.verified_value: StandardCase.bsp is a Blue Sky Plan project file; .bsp is confirmed as Blue Sky Plan's native format
pi_items.source: Blue Sky Plan software documentation and official website (blueskyplan.com); .bsp is Blue Sky Bio's project file format

privileged_info_summary: The task uses synthetic dental CBCT data (PatientID: BSP_TEST_001, PatientName: DENTAL^PHANTOM) with 100 slices in a 102.4 x 102.4 x 40.0 mm volume. The expected output is an STL mandible mesh with height between 30-90 mm. Blue Sky Plan is a verified medical device software for dental CBCT segmentation and STL export. The minimum height of 30 mm is reasonable given typical mandibular variation (ramus height 40-71 mm from literature). However, the maximum height of 90 mm appears incorrect: it exceeds both typical mandible dimensions and the available DICOM scan depth (40 mm z-direction), and contradicts the verifier's internal comment stating mandible-only meshes are 'typically 40-80mm height.'
